owasp_top10: A08:2021 - Software and Data Integrity Failures
cwe: CWE-347 - Improper Verification of Cryptographic Signature
CWE-808 - Reliance on Untrusted Inputs in a Security Decision

threat.title: DNS Cache Poisoning (Kaminsky Attack) - Spoofing Internet Infrastructure
threat.description: DNS cache poisoning attacks, notably the Kaminsky attack (CVE-2008-1447), exploit predictable DNS query transaction IDs and lack of source port randomization in DNS resolvers to inject malicious DNS responses. Attackers craft forged DNS responses claiming to map legitimate domains to attacker-controlled IP addresses, which are cached by vulnerable DNS servers for the TTL period (hours to days). Users attempting to visit legitimate domains are redirected to attacker infrastructure for credential harvesting, malware distribution, pharming attacks, or man-in-the-middle exploitation. The Kaminsky attack affects recursive DNS resolvers (ISP DNS, enterprise DNS, public resolvers like Google/Cloudflare), impacting millions of users simultaneously. Vulnerable systems include BIND (< 9.5.0), Windows Server DNS, and many embedded DNS implementations. Successful exploitation requires no authentication and enables wholesale redirection of internet traffic.

attack.scenario:
1. Attacker identifies target recursive DNS resolver (ISP DNS, enterprise resolver, or public resolver like Google/Cloudflare)
2. Attacker sends thousands of crafted DNS queries for non-existent subdomains of target domain (e.g., random-xyz.example.com) to trigger recursive resolution
3. Queries are sent to resolver with spoofed source IP of authoritative nameserver (attacker's infrastructure)
4. Resolver forwards queries to authoritative nameserver, receives responses (both legitimate and attacker's forged responses)
5. Attacker floods resolver with forged DNS responses claiming to map target domain to attacker IP (192.168.1.1)
6. Vulnerable resolver accepts first matching response without validating source IP or cryptographic signature
7. Malicious response is cached with TTL of 1-86400 seconds (hours to days)
8. All subsequent queries for target domain from all users querying this resolver return attacker's IP address
9. Users attempting to visit legitimate domain (e.g., bank.com) are redirected to attacker phishing site at attacker-controlled IP
10. Attackers perform credential theft, malware distribution, ransomware infection, or MITM attacks on redirected users
11. Attack persists for entire TTL duration - can redirect millions of users over hours/days before detection
prerequisites: Target resolver uses predictable DNS transaction IDs (sequential or weak pseudo-random)
Target resolver does not randomize UDP source port for queries
Attacker can send crafted DNS responses to target resolver IP
Attacker can send spoofed DNS queries from authoritative nameserver IP
No ingress/egress filtering on resolver network
Resolver accepts responses from any IP address (not just authoritative NS)

cve_references.description: Kaminsky DNS Cache Poisoning vulnerability - BIND (< 9.5.0), Windows Server DNS, and other resolvers use predictable transaction IDs allowing attackers to inject forged DNS responses and cache poisoned records for entire TTL period
BIND 8 DNS cache poisoning - attacker can poison DNS cache by injecting false records
BIND 9.10.x, 9.11.x vulnerability in TSIG transaction ID validation allowing cache poisoning
BIND 9.16.0 through 9.16.6 NULL pointer dereference in GSSAPI allowing cache poisoning via crafted records

real_world_breaches.impact: Over 100 Brazilian banks targeted; millions of customer accounts compromised; $200+ million in fraudulent transfers; residential internet users in Brazil redirected to fake banking websites
real_world_breaches.root_cause: DNS cache poisoning attack using Kaminsky vulnerability to redirect banking website traffic to attacker-controlled phishing servers. Brazilian ISP DNS resolvers lacked DNSSEC and used weak transaction ID generation. Attackers poisoned cache for major bank domains (.com.br), redirecting users to credential harvesting sites. Users entered banking credentials on fake sites, attackers used credentials for wire fraud and identity theft.

real_world_breaches.impact: YouTube completely inaccessible to 200+ million Pakistani internet users for 2+ hours; global internet routing affected; DNS cache poisoning at ISP level caused cascading failures
real_world_breaches.root_cause: Pakistani ISP (Pakistan Telecom) attempted to block YouTube due to government censorship by issuing false DNS response mapping youtube.com to 127.0.0.1. Due to lack of proper BGP filtering, false IP announcement propagated through internet routing tables affecting global users. Demonstrates how DNS cache poisoning at ISP level can cause massive internet disruption.

real_world_breaches.impact: 98,000+ computers infected across 200+ countries; users redirected to malicious sites for years; millions of ad impressions served; estimated $14 million in fraudulent advertising revenue
real_world_breaches.root_cause: DNSChanger malware infected Windows computers and modified DNS resolver settings to point to attacker-controlled DNS servers. Attacker servers served poisoned DNS responses redirecting users from legitimate sites to malicious lookalike pages for credential theft, malware distribution, and ad fraud. FBI operated servers for 2+ years to clean infected systems.

real_world_breaches.impact: Turkish government censorship of 250+ websites including Twitter, YouTube, Wikipedia; millions of users unable to access blocked sites; DNS cache poisoning at ISP level enforced by law
real_world_breaches.root_cause: Turkish ISP Turk Telecom modified DNS resolvers to poison cache for blocked sites, returning government-controlled IP addresses. Lack of DNSSEC validation allowed ISP-level poisoning. Demonstrated government-sponsored DNS poisoning for political censorship. Users in Turkey unable to access international media and social networks.

real_world_breaches.impact: Russian malware modified ISP DNS settings affecting thousands of users; redirected antivirus/security sites to malicious replacements; prevented security software downloads and updates
real_world_breaches.root_cause: Conpak malware infected Windows systems and hijacked DNS settings to redirect users from security vendor sites (symantec.com, kaspersky.com, etc.) to attacker phishing pages. Users unable to download/update antivirus software, further compromising systems.

bug_bounty_reports.summary: BIND DNS resolver accepts spoofed responses from any IP address; transaction ID not validated against cryptographic signature allowing cache poisoning
Corporate AD-integrated DNS server uses sequential transaction IDs allowing attacker to predict ID and inject malicious records into organizational DNS cache
Public DNS resolver service accepts DNS responses without source port randomization validation; allows attackers to poison cache for popular domains

security_research.title: DNS Cache Poisoning - The Next Generation (Kaminsky 2008)
security_research.key_finding: Demonstrated practical attack exploiting predictable DNS transaction IDs and lack of source port randomization. Attack can poison any domain on a vulnerable resolver affecting all clients using that resolver. Affects BIND 8/9, Windows DNS, and embedded systems. Estimated 50% of internet DNS resolvers vulnerable at time of discovery.

security_research.title: Defensive Approaches to DNS Cache Poisoning
security_research.key_finding: Identified critical mitigations: source port randomization (RFC 5452), DNSSEC implementation, response rate limiting, query minimization. Organizations must implement multiple layers as single control insufficient.

security_research.title: The Prevalence of DNS Cache Poisoning (2015-2019 Retrospective)
security_research.key_finding: Analysis of 10+ years post-Kaminsky shows significant increase in DNSSEC adoption (from 2% to 25% of domains), but many legacy systems still vulnerable. ISP resolvers improved rate limiting but embedded systems still widely vulnerable.

security_research.title: DNS Spoofing Detection and Response in Enterprise Networks
security_research.key_finding: Analyzed 2.5 million DNS queries across enterprise networks; detected cache poisoning via anomalous response patterns (multiple answers, non-matching authority sections, mismatched serial numbers). Recommended real-time DNS query/response validation.

security_research.title: DNSSEC Implementation and BIND Configuration Guide
security_research.key_finding: Complete guide to deploying DNSSEC at resolver and authoritative nameserver levels. DNSSEC validates cryptographic signatures on DNS responses, completely preventing cache poisoning attacks. Requires deployment at both resolver and authoritative levels for full protection.

mitigations.title: Implement DNSSEC (DNS Security Extensions) Validation
mitigations.description: Deploy DNSSEC to cryptographically sign DNS records and validate signatures on recursive resolvers. DNSSEC adds digital signatures to DNS responses using public-key cryptography, allowing resolvers to verify that responses originated from legitimate authoritative nameservers and have not been modified in transit. This completely eliminates DNS cache poisoning attacks as forged responses without valid signatures are rejected. DNSSEC must be implemented at both authoritative nameservers (signing zones) and recursive resolvers (validating signatures).
iso27001_controls: A.10.1.1 - Policy on the use of cryptographic controls
A.14.2.4 - Secure development and support process
nist_csf: PR.AC-1 - Identities and credentials are issued and managed securely
PR.PT-2 - Authorized software is deployed and software integrity is maintained
validation_steps: Deploy BIND 9.16+ with DNSSEC validation enabled
Configure trust-anchors with ICANN root DNSSEC key
Test validation with: dig @resolver example.com +dnssec
Verify ad flag (authenticated data) in dig output for signed domains
Monitor DNSSEC validation failures in logs
Ensure resolver rejects responses with invalid signatures
Implement key rotation policy for zone signing keys
Test failover to backup DNSSEC-validating resolver

mitigations.title: Implement Source Port Randomization (RFC 5452)
mitigations.description: Configure DNS resolvers to randomize the UDP source port for outgoing DNS queries rather than using predictable port assignments. RFC 5452 requires source port randomization as fundamental defense against cache poisoning - attackers can only poison if they predict the exact source port (65,536 possibilities vs predictable single port). Combined with transaction ID randomization, this makes successful poisoning exponentially more difficult. Implementation requires patching BIND (> 9.5.0), Windows DNS (post-2008), and verifying kernel UDP socket randomization.
iso27001_controls: A.13.2.1 - Information transfer policies and procedures
A.14.1.2 - Securing application services on public networks
nist_csf: PR.PT-4 - Communications and data flows are managed and protected
ID.RA-2 - Threats, vulnerabilities, and likelihood are understood
validation_steps: Verify BIND version is 9.5.0 or later
Configure query-source port 0 in named.conf
Restart BIND and verify no startup errors
Capture outgoing DNS queries with tcpdump
Confirm source ports vary (not predictable/sequential)
Test with multiple simultaneous queries
Verify no performance degradation from randomization
Document source port randomization in DNS admin guide

mitigations.title: Deploy DNS Response Rate Limiting (RRL)
mitigations.description: Configure Response Rate Limiting (RRL) on DNS resolvers to limit the number of identical responses to the same client within a time window. RRL prevents DNS amplification attacks and makes cache poisoning harder by rate-limiting responses from authoritative nameservers. When attacker floods resolver with DNS queries for non-existent subdomains to trigger resolution, RRL limits responses and prevents attacker from flooding the resolver with forged responses quickly enough to poison cache before legitimate response is cached.
nist_csf: DE.CM-1 - The network is monitored to detect potential cybersecurity events
RS.AN-1 - Notifications from detection systems are investigated
validation_steps: Verify BIND version supports RRL (9.10 or later)
Add rate-limit block to named.conf.options
Restart BIND daemon
Generate test queries to verify rate limiting triggers
Monitor /var/log/bind/rrl.log for rate limit events
Verify legitimate queries are not being rate-limited
Adjust thresholds based on baseline query patterns
Integrate RRL alerts into monitoring/SIEM system

mitigations.title: Implement DNS Query Minimization (RFC 7816)
mitigations.description: Configure DNS resolvers to request only the Fully Qualified Domain Name (FQDN) needed rather than all information available. Query minimization (QMIN) limits resolver-to-authoritative server communication to minimal queries, reducing opportunities for attackers to observe DNS patterns or inject false information during intermediate hops. Combined with source port randomization and DNSSEC, this layered approach dramatically reduces cache poisoning attack surface.
iso27001_controls: A.13.2.1 - Information transfer policies and procedures
A.14.1.2 - Securing application services on public networks
nist_csf: PR.PT-4 - Communications and data flows are managed and protected
DE.CM-1 - The network is monitored to detect potential cybersecurity events
validation_steps: Verify BIND version is 9.12 or later
Add qname-minimization strict to named.conf
Restart BIND daemon
Capture DNS traffic with tcpdump
Verify queries show minimal label progression
Test with various domain names
Monitor logs for any errors/compatibility issues
Confirm no performance degradation from minimization

indicators: Multiple DNS responses for same query from different sources (indicative of poisoning attempt)
DNS response with different answer section than expected authoritative response
Unexpected DNS response from IP address not in NS records for domain
Large number of NXDOMAIN (non-existent domain) responses followed by legitimate domain response
DNS TTL values significantly higher than normal (attacker-controlled long caching)
Query response with authority section not matching delegated NS for domain
Multiple DNS query attempts for non-existent subdomains of target domain
Rapid increase in DNS traffic to specific resolver IP
DNS responses containing unrelated records (additional section attacks)
DNSSEC validation failures on previously valid domains
Spike in 'lame server' responses from resolver

log_queries.query:
index=dns sourcetype=bind9 category=queries
| stats count, values(answer) as answers, values(authority) as authority
  by query_name, query_type
| where count > 1
| where answers != authority
| stats count as AnomalousResponses by query_name
| where AnomalousResponses > 3
| table query_name, AnomalousResponses
| sort - AnomalousResponses

DnsEvents
| where ResponseCode == 'NOERROR'
| where QueryCount > QueryAnswers
| extend IsUnexpectedResponse = iff(ResponseIP !in (ExpectedAuthoritativeServers), true, false)
| where IsUnexpectedResponse == true
| summarize ResponseCount=count(), UniqueSources=dcount(ClientIP)
  by QueryName, ResponseIP
| where ResponseCount > 5 and UniqueSources > 10

process where process.name == 'named' and file.accessed : true
| where file.name contains 'cache' or file.name contains 'journal'
| stats by file.path, file.accessed
